request.system: You are a regulatory analyst. Write a 2-3 sentence storyline summary (≤500 chars total) of the regulatory pressure history for this prediction-market contract. For 'retrospective' kind, write past tense and reference the actual resolution. For 'active' kind, write present tense and note what's still pending. Return JSON {text: ...}.
request.user:
Contract: SCOTUS rules Trump can fire FTC commissioners?
Kind: active
Timeline (5 events):
- 2026-05-11 [background] FTC Chairman Ferguson Advises Companies to Comply with the Take It Down Act — FTC chair advising compliance shows agency activity but gives no direct evidence that SCOTUS has ruled the President can remove FTC commissioners.
- 2026-05-04 [background] Protect your business from scams and online threats this National Small Business Week — FTC consumer guidance on scams is unrelated to the Supreme Court removal case and does not affect the chance of a pro‑Trump ruling.
- 2026-05-01 [background] FTC to Host Workshop on “Litigate the Fix” Merger Remedy Proposals — FTC workshop on merger remedies is routine agency activity unrelated to the Supreme Court's removal‑power case; no material effect on YES odds.
- 2026-04-02 [background] Jena Antonucci, In the Matter of — FTC ALJ case (Jena Antonucci) is an administrative enforcement matter unrelated to SCOTUS removal of FTC commissioners; no impact on resolution.
- 2026-03-12 [background] Rule on Unfair or Deceptive Rental Housing Fee Practices — FTC rulemaking shows the agency remains active but does not materially affect the Supreme Court's legal ruling on president's removal power.

response.text: Timeline items show routine FTC rulemaking, guidance, workshops, and enforcement, offering no signal on SCOTUS’s view of presidential removal power over commissioners. The Supreme Court has not ruled yet; resolution awaits any decision clarifying whether a president (including Trump) can fire FTC commissioners.
